title: A fragile deal – but one that reduces the risk of adverse scenarios
gist: Instant reaction to the Trump-announced US-Iran deal: the truce cuts the tail risk of an oil-price spike and global recession, but energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz will only recover gradually (~80% by end-Q3), inflation still rises near-term, and central banks face a hold-then-eventually-unwind path while OECD oil inventory drawdowns keep the energy balance precarious.

themes.theme: Strait of Hormuz energy flow recovery

insights.claim: Before the conflict, roughly 25% of global seaborne oil supply and one-fifth of seaborne natural gas transited the Strait of Hormuz, making its reopening the key swing variable for the global economy.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: The single most important macro lever post-deal is verified tanker throughput through Hormuz; watch shipping/insurance data, not just headlines.
insights.quote: Prior to the conflict, around 25% of global seaborne oil supply and one-fifth of seaborne natural gas passed through the Strait.

insights.claim: Capital Economics' working assumption is that only ~80% of energy flows through the Strait resume by the end of Q3, because tankers are mispositioned, facilities must ramp, and ship-insurance cost/availability remains uncertain.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: A gradual, sub-100% reopening means the oil-price premium fades slowly, not instantly — fade the V-shaped recovery trade.
insights.quote: Our current working assumption is that ~80% of energy flows will resume by the end of Q3.
insights.locator: It is likely that it will take some time

insights.claim: Natural gas flows will recover more slowly than oil because damage to Qatari facilities has knocked an estimated 17% of production offline for two to three years per local officials.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: Structural multi-year LNG supply loss keeps a floor under European/Asian gas prices even if oil normalizes — bullish gas vs oil spread.
insights.quote: according to local officials has put 17% of production offline for two to three years
insights.locator: Natural gas flows will be slower to return

insights.claim: Even with an immediate reopening, inflation still rises further near-term and Q3 sees economic damage, but the world avoids recession — growth dips below trend in Q3 then recovers to a pre-conflict pace of just over 3% in late 2026 into 2027.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: Base case is a growth scare and slowdown, not a recession — supports risk assets on a 6-12 month horizon if the deal holds.
insights.quote: Rather than a recession, the global economy will probably face a period of below-trend growth in Q3, before world GDP growth recovers to its pre-conflict pace of just over 3% in late 2026 and into 2027.
insights.locator: Even if the deal reopens the Strait immediately

insights.claim: The Gulf states are the key downside exception, facing what Capital Economics calls the largest GDP downturn since the 1980s as a result of the conflict.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: Regional EM/GCC equity and credit exposure carries idiosyncratic recession risk distinct from the constructive global call.
insights.quote: the hit to their economies so far will lead to the largest downturn in GDP since the 1980s
insights.locator: The Gulf states are a key exception

insights.claim: The deal makes a Fed and Bank of England hold this week a near-certainty, in contrast to the ECB which tightened last week, and may cancel an anticipated Indonesia rate hike while inclining the Czech central bank to hold.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: Reduced near-term hike pressure across DM and EM is a tailwind for front-end rates; position for fewer hikes than feared.
insights.quote: a rate hike that we had anticipated this week in Indonesia may now not happen and the Czech central bank will probably be more inclined to hold too
insights.locator: Central banks under less pressure to raise rates

insights.claim: Inflation is set to stay above target in most major economies through this year and into the first half of next even as growth stays weak, leaving central banks in a difficult stagflationary navigation.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: Above-target inflation with weak growth caps how dovish central banks can be — a difficult backdrop for both bonds and cyclical equities.
insights.quote: with inflation set to stay above target in most major economies throughout this year and the first half of next, even as growth remains relatively weak
insights.locator: The outlook will nonetheless remain a difficult one

insights.claim: Policymakers' fear of repeating the 2022 mistake of staying too loose for too long means further modest 'insurance hikes' remain possible, more likely from the ECB than the Bank of England.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: Lingering hawkish bias driven by 2022 reputational scarring keeps a residual hike risk priced — ECB hike risk > BoE.
insights.quote: policymakers will not want to make the same mistake as then, when they were widely criticised for keeping policy too loose for too long

insights.claim: Once energy-driven inflation fades next year rate hikes are likely to be unwound, and Capital Economics judges that market-implied DM rate paths for 2027-28 still look too high.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: A receive-rates / long duration view for 2027-28: the market is pricing too high a terminal/forward path versus CE's call.
insights.quote: the expected path of interest rates across DMs in 2027-28 still looks too high

themes.theme: Precarious energy balance and tail risk

insights.claim: Oil and gas prices rose less than feared mainly because of a large inventory drawdown across major advanced economies and China, masking the underlying tightness rather than resolving it.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: The muted price reaction is borrowed time from inventories, not slack — the supply cushion is thinning, raising spike risk.
insights.quote: this has been due in part to a large drawdown in inventories in major advanced economies and China
insights.locator: Energy markets still in a precarious balance

insights.claim: The current OECD oil-stock drawdown pace would leave commercial inventories at critically low levels by end-July, levels historically consistent with oil prices of $130-150 per barrel.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: A concrete dated trigger and price target for the bear case: end-July OECD stocks are the key tape to watch for a $130-150 oil scenario.
insights.quote: the current pace of drawdown would leave commercial oil stocks in OECD countries at critically low levels by the end of July – and levels that have previously been consistent with prices in the range of $130-150pb
insights.locator: Indeed, the available data from OECD countries

insights.claim: In the adverse scenario Europe slips into a mild recession, Asian growth weakens sharply, and advanced-economy inflation moves back above 5%, making it hard for central banks to look through the shock and pushing them toward further tightening that worsens the downturn.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: Maps the explicit tail: 5%+ inflation forces hikes into a downturn — a stagflation/policy-mistake scenario to hedge if the Strait stays constrained.
insights.quote: Europe would probably slip into a mild recession, growth across Asia would weaken sharply and inflation in advanced economies would move back above 5%.
insights.locator: The macroeconomic consequences in such a scenario

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh's debut as Fed Chair frames this week's FOMC, with Capital Economics arguing the rationale is to hold even though the committee was turning more hawkish ahead of the Iran deal.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: New-chair signaling risk at a hawkish-leaning FOMC is a key US event; a hold despite hawkish drift is the CE base case.

insights.claim: A Bank of Japan rate hike this week is treated as baked in, and strengthening inflation pressures could accelerate the pace of tightening beyond the June meeting.
insights.speaker: Neil Shearing
insights.so_what: BoJ is the DM hawkish outlier — a faster tightening path supports the yen and pressures JGBs versus consensus.
insights.quote: A Bank of Japan rate hike this week is baked in, and strengthening inflation pressures could accelerate the pace of tightening beyond the June meeting.
insights.locator: A Bank of Japan rate hike this week
